h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to simply draw the bar line after the third quarter note as instructed by the tutor.

scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think it is because `B` and `3` tell the place on the grid, but I want to try the next line before I say I really have it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to try to fill both the address and value boxes for the next cell.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Address box: `C4`, because that is column `C` and row `4`. Value box: I’m leaving it blank for now, because I still do not see the number inside that cell.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner successfully executes the 'evidence gate' procedure by correctly identifying the address and leaving the value blank due to lack of visual evidence.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Column plus row settles only the address box; the value box needs visible evidence from inside the cell. Use an evidence gate on this line: mark `address` as “column + row” and fill `C4`; mark `value` as “visible cell only” and leave it blank until `C4` is on screen.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor recognizes the learner's blockage regarding the missing value and introduces the 'evidence gate' mechanism to separate address-determination from value-copying.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Use an evidence gate on this line: mark `address` as “column + row” and fill `C4`; mark `value` as “visible cell only” and leave it blank until `C4` is on screen.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The 'evidence gate' is a highly precise, actionable constraint that perfectly addresses the learner's confusion by separating the structural address from the empirical

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to struggle with the competing visual cues of the pencil mark and the plastic arrow.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Friction stays on this left plastic arrow: the surface acts on the cart that way. The pencil mark is the cart’s travel mark, so it does not receive the force tile. For push, the tile has to go on the plastic arrow for the hand’s force, not just where the cart is moving.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner successfully resolves the visual conflict and correctly applies the force rule to both friction and push.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The old rule is not settling this because two marks are competing. Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Put the friction tile on the visible arrow that qualifies. Then lift the strip and name the pencil mark’s job without moving the tile.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor diagnoses the conflict between competing marks and introduces a physical paper strip as a visual gate to scaffold the task.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Use this gate first: only marks visible above the strip can receive force labels. Put the friction tile on the visible arrow that qualifies. Then lift the strip and name the pencil mark’s job without moving the tile.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The paper strip is a highly effective physical tool that isolates the target variable, enabling the learner to resolve the visual conflict and generalize the rule.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, so no recognition-contingent tutor uptake is possible.
